plan.description: This is the data management plan of the project entitled ‘Analysis of the nasopharyngeal microbiome, virological, immune and inflammatory factors associated with slow clinical stabilisation and poor outcome in adults hospitalised with influenza and respiratory syncytial virus infection’. - MiViFlu-RSV.
plan.title: PGD_MiViFlu_RSV

reference.label: Fundación Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria y Biomédica de Alicante - Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria y Biomédica de Alicante (ISABIAL)

description.description:
The MiViFlu_RSV project will collect and store data from demographic variables (sex, age, place of residence...), clinical variables related to the respiratory infection under study (admission, mechanical ventilation...), and with different explanatory variables related to the patient's health and relevant for obtaining results (diabetes, frailty, dependence...).
In addition, data will be generated related to the microbiome of the patient (Shannon index, % of filos abudance...) and to the pathogen causing the respiratory infection (variant, subtype, viral load...).
description.label: MiViFlu_RSV Data

descriptionTemplate.label: CHIST-ERA Data Management - Generic Questions
descriptionTemplate.description: This is a DMP template created based on the CHIST-ERA RDM policy and DMP template.

section.title: Documentation And Data Quality

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be organised during the project
fieldSet.description: Νaming conventions should be clearly described in order to make data understandable by other researchers that may reuse them after the project ends. Μentioning for example conventions, version control, and folder structures. consistent, well-ordered research data will be easier to find, understand, and re-use.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Check out some examples of file naming best practices: http://tiny.cc/op8lcz

labelData.label: Please provide information and links to the documentation, should it is available

fieldSet.title: Consider what other documentation is needed to enable re-use
fieldSet.description: This may include information on the methodology used to collect the data, analytical and procedural information, definitions of variables, units of measurement, and so on.

referenceTypeData.label: Select the tools used for this activity

fieldSet.title: Consider how this information will be captured and where it will be recorded
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For example in a database with links to each item, a ‘readme’ text file, file headers, code books, or lab notebooks.

section.description: For example the methodology of data collection and way of organizing data
section.title: What metadata and documentation will accompany the data?

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how the consistency and quality of data collection will be controlled and documented
fieldSet.description: Quality assurance procedures are closely related to RDM and are meant to validate that data are clean (no duplicates or inconsistencies), error-free, well structured and represented in analysis. Quality assurance checks can be facilitated by the use of tools or scripts.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To familiarise yourself with the process, you may find a checklist here: https://www.eur.nl/sites/corporate/files/2017-11/Checklist_QA_QC_RDM_0_2_MD_20150129.pdf

section.title: What data quality control measures will be used?

fieldSet.title: State any constraints on re-use of existing data if there are any
Briefly state the reasons if the re-use of any existing data sources has been considered but discarded

section.title: Reused Data

page.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

section.title: Storage And Backup During The Research Process

referenceTypeData.label: Select the storage resource that was used for this activity

fieldSet.title: Describe where the data will be stored and backed up during research activities and how often the backup will be performed
fieldSet.description: It is recommended to store data in least at two separate locations. Give preference to the use of robust, managed storage with automatic backup, such as provided by IT support services of the home institution. Storing data on laptops, stand-alone hard drives, or external storage devices such as USB sticks is not recommended.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To learn more about best practices for back up and data security, you may have a look at: https://www.openaire.eu/raw-data-backup-and-versioning

section.title: How will data and metadata be stored and backed up during the research?

fieldSet.title: Explain who will have access to the data during the research and how access to data is controlled, especially in collaborative partnerships
Explain which institutional data protection policies are in place
fieldSet.additionalInformation: You can select more than one institutions/researchers

section.title: How will data security and protection of sensitive data be taken care of during the research?

page.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

section.title: Legal And Ethical Requirements, Codes Of Conduct

labelData.label: How will compliance with legislation on personal data and on security be ensured?

fieldSet.title: Are there any personal data to be formulated?
fieldSet.description: It is possible that data are sensitive, such as personal or confidential, and hence they can not be shared directly to others. For those types of data, specific processes need to be made in order to ensure their reliable exchange in the short and long-term.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Find out more about sensitive data and how to process them: https://www.openaire.eu/sensitive-data-guide

fieldSet.title: Explain whether there is a managed access procedure in place for authorised users of personal data

section.description: Ensure that when dealing with personal data data protection laws (for example GDPR) are complied with: ›› Gain informed consent for preservation and/or sharing of personal data. ›› Consider anonymisation of personal data for preservation and/or sharing (truly anonymous data are no longer considered personal data). ›› Consider pseudonymisation of personal data (the main difference with anonymisation is that pseudonymisation is reversible). ›› Consider encryption which is seen as a special case of pseudonymisation (the encryption key must be stored separately from the data, for instance by a trusted third party).
section.title: Personal data
How will other legal issues, such as intellectual property rights and ownership, be managed? What legislation is applicable?

fieldSet.title: Who will be the owner(s) of the data?
fieldSet.description: Meaning who will have the rights to control access.

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer
Please give more information, including links to specific documents (if available)

fieldSet.title: Explain what access or restrictions will apply to the data?

section.description: Make sure to cover these matters of rights to control access to data for multi-partner projects and multiple data owners, in the consortium agreement.
section.title: Data ownership and accessibility

fieldSet.title: Explain which intellectual property and how will they be dealt with

section.title: Intellectual property rights

fieldSet.title: Are there any restrictions on the re-use of third-party data?

section.title: Third-party data restrictions

options.label: Legal issues such as IP rights
options.value: Legal issues such as IP rights

options.label: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species
options.value: Environmental issues such as the locating of endangered species

options.label: Security issues for organisations or countries
options.value: Security issues for organisations or countries

fieldSet.title: What ethical issues and codes of conduct are there, and how will they be taken into account?

section.description:
Consider whether ethical issues can affect how data are stored and transferred, who can see or use them, and how long they are kept. Demonstrate awareness of these aspects and respective planning. 
Follow the national and international codes of conducts and institutional ethical guidelines, and check if ethical review (for example by an ethics committee) is required for data collection in the research project.
section.title: Ethical issues
Data Sharing And Long-term Preservation

options.label: Deposit in a FAIR-enabling data repository
options.value: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

options.label: Use of a secure data service
options.value: Use of a secure data service

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how the data will be discoverable and shared
fieldSet.description: Please note that “shared” refers to sharing with people outside the Project consortium.

fieldSet.title: Outline the plan for data preservation and give information on how long the data will be retained
Explain when the data will be made available
Will exclusive use of the data be claimed?
Indicate whether and for how long data sharing will be embargoed or restricted
fieldSet.extendedDescription: For example to publish, protect intellectual property, or seek patents.

fieldSet.title: Indicate how the data will be used
Is it necessary to restrict access to certain communities or to apply a data sharing agreement?

section.description: Please check with funders' or institutions's policies which might apply an embargo on data access.
section.title: How and when will data be shared? Are there possible restrictions to data sharing or embargo reasons?

options.label: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)
options.value: Observational (e.g., sensor data, data from surveys)

options.label: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)
options.value: Derived or compiled (e.g., text mining, 3D models)

options.label: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)
options.value: Reference or canonical (e.g., static, peer-reviewed data sets, likely published or curated, such as gene sequence databanks or chemical structures)

fieldSet.title: Describe the data to be preserved long-term
Indicate where the data will be deposited
fieldSet.description: Select a repository that will host the research data outputs for long-term preservation.

fieldSet.title: How will the data be effectively curated beyond the lifetime of the grant
fieldSet.description: It is recommended to demonstrate that the repositories policies and procedures (including any metadata standards, and costs involved) have been checked.

section.description: For example a data repository or archive.
section.title: How will data for preservation be selected, and where data will be preserved long-term?

fieldSet.title: What type of persistent identifier (PID) will be used?
fieldSet.description:
Persistent identifiers should be applied so that data can be reliably and efficiently located and referred to. Persistent identifiers also help to track citations and re-use. A persistent identifier or PID is a long-lasting reference to a resource. Whatever resource it refers to, the primary purpose of the PID is to provide the information required to reliably identify, verify and locate it. A PID may be connected to a set of metadata describing an item rather than to the item itself.
Typically, a trustworthy, long-term repository will provide a persistent identifier.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: Learn more about PIDs and how to use them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-can-identifiers-improve-the-dissemination-of-your-research-outputs

section.title: How will the application of a unique and persistent identifier to each data set be ensured?
Data Management Responsibilities And Resources

fieldSet.title: Outline the roles and responsibilities for data management/stewardship activities
fieldSet.extendedDescription:
For example:
  • data capture, 
  • metadata production, 
  • data quality, 
  • storage and backup, 
  • data archiving, and 
  • data sharing.

fieldSet.title: Explain the co-ordination of data management responsibilities across partners

section.description: Provide names and responsibilities of researchers’ or data managers’ data management and stewardship activities that are performed throughout the project. Follow the research data management lifecycle to guide your steps in data capture, metadata production, data quality, storage and backup, data archiving, and data sharing.
section.title: Who will be responsible for data management?

fieldSet.title: Explain how the necessary resources to prepare the data for sharing/preservation have been costed in
fieldSet.description: Research Data Management is not a costless procedure, so it is important that it is thought through at the planning stage of research. Carefully consider and justify any resources needed to deliver the data. These may include storage costs, hardware, staff time, costs of preparing data for deposit, and repository charges.
fieldSet.extendedDescription: To understand what costs are allocated across the steps of a RDM lifecycle, how to estimate them and how to prevent them: https://www.openaire.eu/how-to-comply-to-h2020-mandates-rdm-costs

fieldSet.title: Indicate whether additional resources will be needed to prepare data for deposit or to meet any charges from data repositories

labelData.label: Please provide more details on your answer

fieldSet.title: Explain how much cost provisionally is needed
fieldSet.description: You can use: Data Management Costing Tool (zingtree.com)

section.title: What resources will be dedicated to data management and ensuring that data will be FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Re-usable)?

reference.label: Fundación Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria y Biomédica de Alicante - Instituto de Investigación Sanitaria y Biomédica de Alicante (ISABIAL)

field.textValue: ISABIAL guarantees compliance with Organic Law 3/2018 and Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR). The data will be anonymized, avoiding identifiable information, and in the case of sensitive variables, will be managed exclusively by the IP/CoIP, with guidance from ISABIAL's data protection officer.

The data entered in the eCRD will be anonymized from the outset, meaning there will be no information that allows the identification of patients in the eCRD. However, access to RedCap will be through user credentials, and only the IP/CoIP will have access to data from all centers. The other involved centers will only be able to consult the data entered by their own center.
All variables will be recorded in the eCRD generated in RedCap.

The person responsible for the data at each center will be the investigator designated by the Principal Investigator (PI) at each center.

The researchers responsible for the data contained in the complete eCRD will be the Principal Investigators (PIs) of the project, advised by the data management officer of ISABIAL. Ultimately, the final responsibility for data management will rest with the Institution.

Data processing will be conducted exclusively for research purposes, in accordance with Articles 6.1 and 9.2 of the GDPR and Additional Provision 17.2.d of the LOPD-GDD. Pseudonymized data will only be accessible to authorized research personnel.

The data obtained through experimental procedures using microbiome sequencing and whole genome sequencing will be deposited in FASTQ/FASTA format in the NCBI repository, specifying the type of samples from which these files have been obtained. A reduced set of metadata, approved by the ISABIAL Ethics Committee, will be available upon request, with justification of the need and approval from the ISABIAL Ethics Committee

he RedCap software, in which the data will be collected, is installed on a computer server at ISABIAL, which is part of the computer network of the Hospital General Universitario Dr. Balmis in Alicante. This, in turn, is connected to the network of the Generalitat Valenciana, ensuring that it is equipped with the full cybersecurity measures provided by being part of a public organization within the healthcare network.

he project will be submitted to the Ethics Committee for Research with Medicinal Products (CEIm) for approval. The project includes a Patient Information Sheet, and informed consent will be obtained from the patients participating. Additionally, the project will comply with the guidelines established in the Declaration of Helsinki (2000), with Organic Law 3/2018, and with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR).

Access to the RedCap eCRD will be protected by credentials (username and password). Users will be generated at the request of the IP/CoIP and always under justification of the need. Groups of users will be generated for each centre, restricting the visibility of each centre's data to its centre. 

Access to the full database data will be limited to the principal investigator and co-principal investigator.

ISABIAL guarantees compliance with Organic Law 3/2018 and Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR). The data will be anonymized, avoiding identifiable information, and in the case of sensitive variables, will be managed exclusively by the IP/CoIP, with guidance from ISABIAL's data protection officer.

In the case of the variables collected from the patient's clinical history and entered by the researchers in the eCRD hosted in RedCap, different quality controls are carried out: 

- Data validation: RedCap allows the addition of different types of data validation, for example: correct dates, integers, numerical limits...
- Data review: the main researchers of the project will perform a review of the data entered to assess the cohesiveness of the data. 
- Query system: RedCap has implemented a system of data curation and generation of queries that are sent directly to the researcher responsible for the introduction of the data.
IP - Management of Clinical Data
Co-IP - Management of Experimental Data (genomic)
Co-IP - Management of Experimental Data (genomic)

The data will be organised through the RedCap software (Harris et al, J Biomed Inform 2009 and Harris et al, J Biomed Inform 2019), which will be used to generate the electronic data record (eCRD).

The designation of variable names will be carried out according to the following scheme: 

MiViFlu_TypeOfVariable_nameOfVariable. 

Example result variable  1: 
MiViFlu_VR1_Exitus

Example result variable 2:
MiViFlu_VR2_HRS

Example primary explicative variable:
MiViFlu_VE1_Shannon

Example secundary explicative variable:
MiViFlu_VE2_Diabetes

textListValues.textListValue: Deposit in a trustworthy data repository

field.textValue: ISABIAL has two computer servers and a data storage cabin in the Data Unit of ISABIAL. RedCap is installed on one of the computer servers, from which all its data is backed up quarterly in the storage cabin.
The files obtained from the massive sequencing of the microbiome and complete viral genomes will be deposited in the NCBI thematic repository.

The data obtained from the microbiome and variables related to the pathogen causing the virus will be made available without restriction to the entire scientific community. Only the minimum metadata required for other researchers to use this dataset will be published openly.

Clinical variables corresponding to patients will be restricted.

The files obtained from the massive sequencing of the microbiome and complete viral genomes will remain embargoed until the publication of the scientific article and the presentation of the patent (whichever occurs first).

It is possible that this project could lead to the development of a rapid microbiological diagnostic method based on massive sequencing of the microbiome and complete viral genomes, which could be protected through the generation of a patent, to be managed by ISABIAL.

Information on outcome variables 1, 2 and 3 and secondary explanatory variables will be obtained through the patient's electronic medical record, and will be collected in a fully anonymised form, giving each patient the code generated by RedCap. 

The primary explanatory variables will be generated through experimental procedures and will be attached as metadata to the outcome variables.
